explicit_text: My family moved to Jacksonville, Florida from Flint, Michigan in 1870 when I was very young. We initially lived in South Jacksonville with the family of ex-Governor Harrison Reed. Jacksonville grew rapidly during my father's time at Barnett Bank, and I witnessed events like the Great Fire of 1901 from the south side of the St. Johns River. Over the years, I saw the city develop, including the laying out of Gibson Street near our home.
implicit_text: My family moved to a rapidly growing city in northeastern Florida from Flint, Michigan in 1870 when I was very young. We initially lived in the southern part of the city with the family of a former territorial governor. I witnessed events like the Great Fire of 1901 from the south side of the St. Johns River. Over the years, I saw the city develop, including the laying out of Gibson Street near our home.

explicit_text: My family originally came from Flint, Michigan, where my father was part of the fourth generation of Gibsons settled there. We left Flint in 1870 when my father was ordered to Florida for his health. Though we were considered Yankees in Florida, our roots in Flint were strong and part of our family identity.

explicit_text: Harrison Reed was the first Territorial Governor of Florida and a fellow Yankee from Milwaukee, Wisconsin. My family boarded with his family in South Jacksonville when we first arrived. His son, Harrison Reed Jr., and I were close playmates as children. Governor Reed was also brother to Mrs. Alexander Mitchell, linking our families socially.

explicit_text: Milwaukee, Wisconsin, was the original home of Governor Harrison Reed and Mrs. Alexander Mitchell, who were siblings. explicit_text: My father secured a position as a bookkeeper at Barnett Bank shortly after we arrived in Jacksonville. He was the third man employed there, working alongside B. Barnett and Mr. Cooper. The bank was located at Forsyth and Main Street and grew significantly over the years, employing hundreds by the time of my father's death in 1911, when he was assistant cashier.

explicit_text: Forsyth Street was the location of the Barnett Bank where my father worked. The bank was on the corner of Forsyth and Main Street, a central spot in Jacksonville. This area was part of the city's commercial heart during my youth.

explicit_text: Main Street, known also as Duval Street, intersected with Forsyth Street where the Barnett Bank was located. This intersection was a key part of Jacksonville's downtown area. I remember it as the place where my father worked and where the bank grew from a small firm to a major institution.

explicit_text: Alexander Mitchell was my great-grandfather, and his name was given to the son of David Mitchell and Kittie Parrott. The young Alexander was born in 1900 and was cared for by my mother at times. He later worked in the bond department of the Barnett Bank and inherited a residence on A. Johns Avenue from his grandmother, Mrs. Lint Parrett.

explicit_text: Villa Alexandria was the large, rambling three-story home owned by Mrs. Alexander Mitchell on A. Johns Avenue. I often stayed there as a child and was fascinated by its beautiful French, Japanese, and antique rooms. The house overlooked the St. Johns River and was filled with fine furnishings and European paintings, reflecting Mrs. Mitchell's cultured and artistic tastes.

explicit_text: A. Johns Avenue was where Mrs. Alexander Mitchell's Villa Alexandria was located, farther out on the south side of Jacksonville. It was also the site of the residence left to Alexander Mitchell by his grandmother, Mrs. Lint Parrett. This avenue was part of the Jacksonville suburb of Avondale and represented a refined residential area.

explicit_text: The St. Johns River was a central feature of life in Jacksonville and South Jacksonville. Our family property extended to the river's edge, and I remember crossing it by rowboat daily. From the roof of our house, I could watch the Great Fire of 1901 across the river, including the burning of the Catholic Church. The river was also the site of early ferryboats like the Armington and Mechanic, which connected the two cities.

explicit_text: David Mitchell was the grandson of Mrs. Alexander Mitchell and came to South Jacksonville around 1893 as a young boy. He was known for his spoiled and wild behavior, including riding polo ponies and gambling. After a serious polo accident left him incapacitated, his wife Kittie Parrott eventually divorced him. Despite his troubles, he was devoted to his grandmother and fathered Alexander Mitchell.

explicit_text: Kittie Parrott, originally Kittie Sutton, was adopted by Mr. Parrott and married David Mitchell. She was determined to marry the richest man in Jacksonville and set her sights on David. After his accident, she refused to live with him and later divorced him, causing local scandal before eventually remarrying and settling in West Palm Beach.

explicit_text: I attended the Bradford Institute, a private school in Jacksonville operated by Mrs. Drow Williams under the Episcopal Church. To get there from the south side, I would take the ferryboat Armington across the St. Johns River. The school was an important part of my early education.

explicit_text: I vividly remember the Great Fire of 1901, which started around noon on May 3rd. From the roof of our house on the south side of the river, my siblings and I watched the flames and smoke engulf much of Jacksonville, including the Catholic Church. The fire destroyed many homes and businesses, and our family took in nineteen guests that night. My father stayed at the Barnett Bank until early the next morning, as the bank narrowly escaped the fire.
